title: Introduction to FastSurvival
text: FastSurvival provides fast alternatives standard survival analysis functions survival package, together simulation layer designing evaluating time--event trials. Every function designed repeated evaluation inside large simulation loops, iterative object-building overhead standard implementations becomes bottleneck. Core computations implemented C++ via Rcpp.
code: library(FastSurvival)

title: Introduction to FastSurvival
text: package two families functions. estimation testing functions operate single dataset return S3 object print() method. survfit_fast() evaluates Kaplan-Meier estimate single time point. survdiff_fast() computes log-rank test weighted stratified variants. coxph_fast() returns closed-form hazard ratio. rmst_fast() returns restricted mean survival time. milestone_fast() compares survival milestone timepoint. maxcombo_fast() computes max-combo test. rmw_fast() computes robust modestly-weighted log-rank test. ahsw_fast() computes average hazard survival weight. ahr_fast() computes Kalbfleisch-Prentice average hazard ratio. simulation functions support full simulation study. simdata_fast() generates individual patient data one- two-group trials. analysis_fast() performs interim sequential analyses simulated data one looks. simsummary_fast() aggregates operating characteristics analysis output supplied boundaries.

title: Introduction to FastSurvival
text: following example uses ovarian dataset survival package.
code: library(survival)  # Single-time-point Kaplan-Meier estimate ord <- order(ovarian$futime) survfit_fast(ovarian$futime[ord], ovarian$fustat[ord],              t_eval = 500, conf.type = "log") #> Kaplan-Meier survival estimate (single time point) #>  #>         survival std.err lower 95% upper 95% #> t = 500   0.5961  0.0999    0.4291    0.8279 #>  #>  Confidence interval type: log  # Log-rank test survdiff_fast(ovarian$futime, ovarian$fustat, ovarian$rx,               control = 1, side = 2) #> Log-rank test (two-group) #>  #>   N = 26 #>  #>           Observed Expected (O-E)^2/E (O-E)^2/V #> control          7   5.2335    0.5962    1.0627 #> treatment        5   6.7665    0.4612    1.0627 #>  #>  Chi-square = 1.063 on 1 df,  p-value = 0.3026  # Hazard ratio via the Pike-Halley Estimator coxph_fast(ovarian$futime, ovarian$fustat, ovarian$rx, control = 1) #> Pike-Halley estimator for the hazard ratio (two-group) #>  #> Coefficients: #>          coef exp(coef) se(coef)      z Pr(>|z|) #> group -0.5964    0.5508   0.5868 -1.016     0.31 #>  #> Hazard ratio and 95% Wald confidence interval: #>       exp(coef) exp(-coef) lower .95 upper .95 #> group    0.5508     1.8155    0.1744    1.7399

title: Group sequential design with the simulation trio
text: consider two-group trial one-sided test level 0.025, three equally spaced looks information fraction, O’Brien-Fleming spending function efficacy boundary. first obtain design gsDesign. boundaries increasing stringency earlier looks, expected O’Brien-Fleming spending function. compare boundaries Z scale.
code: library(gsDesign)  k     <- 3 alpha <- 0.025  gsd <- gsDesign(   k         = k,   test.type = 1,   alpha     = alpha,   sfu       = sfLDOF,   timing    = c(1, 2, 3) / 3 )  # Upper (efficacy) Z boundaries at the three looks z_bounds <- gsd$upper$bound z_bounds #> [1] 3.710303 2.511427 1.993048

title: Group sequential design with the simulation trio
text: null hypothesis two groups share hazard. simulate many trials, planned accrual follow-, analyze three event-count looks corresponding information fractions . target final event count chosen design, interim looks one third two thirds . cumulative rejection probability final look estimates type error. close nominal 0.025.
code: nsim         <- 2000 final_events <- 300 event_looks  <- round(final_events * c(1, 2, 3) / 3)  df_null <- simdata_fast(   nsim     = nsim,   n        = c(350, 350),   a.time   = c(0, 12),   a.rate   = 700 / 12,   e.hazard = list(0.05, 0.05),   seed     = 101 )  res_null <- analysis_fast(   df_null, control = 1,   event.looks = event_looks,   stat = "logrank", side = 1 )  sum_null <- simsummary_fast(   res_null,   eff.col   = "logrank.z",   efficacy  = -z_bounds,   direction = "lower" ) sum_null #> Sequential analysis summary (simsummary_fast) #>   Simulations: 2000 #>   Mode: Z-scale boundaries, direction = lower #>     efficacy [logrank.z]: -3.710, -2.511, -1.993 #>  #>     look look.value n.enrolled.mean n.event.mean n.dropout.mean n.pipeline.mean #>        1        100        517.5325       100.00              0        417.5325 #>        2        200        699.8730       200.00              0        499.8730 #>        3        300        700.0000       300.00              0        400.0000 #>  overall         NA        700.0000       299.35              0        400.6500 #>  cutoff.mean prob.stop.efficacy prob.stop.futility prob.stop.any cum.reject #>       8.8672             0.0000                  0        0.0000     0.0000 #>      13.0276             0.0065                  0        0.0065     0.0065 #>      17.4987             0.0150                  0        0.0150     0.0215 #>      17.4706             0.0215                  0        0.0215     0.0215 overall_null <- sum_null[sum_null$look == "overall", ] data.frame(   source = c("simulation", "gsDesign"),   alpha  = c(overall_null$cum.reject, alpha) ) #>       source  alpha #> 1 simulation 0.0215 #> 2   gsDesign 0.0250

title: Speed comparison
text: FastSurvival designed repeated evaluation inside large simulation loops. vignette shows benchmark function standard counterpart reports representative results. benchmark code shown executed vignette built, timing many microbenchmark replicates exceed build-time limits. reproduce numbers, run code blocks interactively. reported figures median times microbenchmark replicates single desktop machine. Absolute timings depend hardware, sample size, event rate, ratios matter raw values.
code: library(FastSurvival) library(survival) library(microbenchmark) # Comparison packages used in the benchmarks below library(survRM2) library(nph)

title: Speed comparison
text: key speed gain analysis functions accept pre-sorted vectors. Inside simulation loop data sorted reused, sort cost paid single time rather every call. build single phase-3-sized dataset benchmarks.
code: set.seed(1) n  <- 600 tt <- rexp(n, rate = 0.05) ev <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.8) gp <- rep(1:2, each = n / 2)  ord <- order(tt) t_s <- tt[ord] e_s <- ev[ord] g_s <- gp[ord]

title: Speed comparison
code: microbenchmark(   fast = survfit_fast(t_s, e_s, t_eval = 20, presorted = TRUE),   base = summary(survfit(Surv(tt, ev) ~ 1), times = 20),   times = 1000 )

title: Speed comparison
code: microbenchmark(   fast = survdiff_fast(t_s, e_s, g_s, control = 1, side = 2, presorted = TRUE),   base = survdiff(Surv(tt, ev) ~ gp),   times = 1000 )

title: Speed comparison
code: microbenchmark(   fast = coxph_fast(t_s, e_s, g_s, control = 1, presorted = TRUE),   base = coxph(Surv(tt, ev) ~ I(gp == 2)),   times = 1000 )

title: Speed comparison
code: arm <- as.integer(gp == 2) microbenchmark(   fast = rmst_fast(t_s, e_s, g_s, control = 1, tau = 20, presorted = TRUE),   base = survRM2::rmst2(time = tt, status = ev, arm = arm, tau = 20),   times = 1000 )

title: Speed comparison
code: microbenchmark(   fast = survdiff_fast(t_s, e_s, g_s, control = 1, side = 2,                        weight = "fh", rho = 0, gamma = 1, presorted = TRUE),   base = nph::logrank.test(tt, ev, gp, rho = 0, gamma = 1),   times = 1000 )

title: Validation of FastSurvival
text: FastSurvival built speed, fast estimator useful returns answer established implementation. vignette checks numerical agreement FastSurvival function reference well-known package: survival package Kaplan-Meier estimate, log-rank test, Cox hazard ratio, milestone survival; survRM2 restricted mean survival time; survAH average hazard survival weight. weighted log-rank max-combo tests checked nph package, robust modestly-weighted test nphRCT package. comparisons form basis automated test suite shipped package.
code: library(FastSurvival)

title: Validation of FastSurvival
text: survfit_fast() evaluates Kaplan-Meier estimate single time point. compare summary(survfit(...)) time ovarian dataset.
code: library(survival)  ord <- order(ovarian$futime) t_s <- ovarian$futime[ord] e_s <- ovarian$fustat[ord]  fast <- survfit_fast(t_s, e_s, t_eval = 500, conf.type = "log")  fit  <- survfit(Surv(futime, fustat) ~ 1, data = ovarian) ref  <- summary(fit, times = 500)  data.frame(   quantity = c("survival", "std.err"),   fast     = c(unclass(fast)["surv"], unclass(fast)["std.err"]),   survival = c(ref$surv, ref$std.err),   row.names = NULL ) #>   quantity       fast   survival #> 1 survival 0.59607843 0.59607843 #> 2  std.err 0.09992615 0.09992615

title: Validation of FastSurvival
text: survdiff_fast() returns two-sided chi-square statistic side = 2, matching statistic survdiff().
code: fast_lr <- survdiff_fast(ovarian$futime, ovarian$fustat, ovarian$rx,                          control = 1, side = 2)  ref_lr  <- survdiff(Surv(futime, fustat) ~ rx, data = ovarian)  c(fast = as.numeric(fast_lr), survival = ref_lr$chisq) #>     fast survival  #>  1.06274  1.06274

title: Validation of FastSurvival
text: survdiff_fast() also computes Fleming-Harrington G(rho, gamma) weighted log-rank tests weight = "fh" argument. nph package provides logrank.test() family, compare chi-square statistic across several weight choices ovarian data. two implementations agree numerical precision across weight family, including ordinary log-rank test recovered rho = 0, gamma = 0.
code: fh_grid <- data.frame(rho = c(0, 1, 0, 1), gamma = c(1, 0, 0, 1))  do.call(rbind, lapply(seq_len(nrow(fh_grid)), function(i) {   r <- fh_grid$rho[i]   g <- fh_grid$gamma[i]   fast <- as.numeric(survdiff_fast(ovarian$futime, ovarian$fustat, ovarian$rx,                                    control = 1, side = 2,                                    weight = "fh", rho = r, gamma = g))   nph_chisq <- nph::logrank.test(ovarian$futime, ovarian$fustat, ovarian$rx,                                  rho = r, gamma = g)$test$Chisq   data.frame(rho = r, gamma = g, fast = fast, nph = nph_chisq) })) #>   rho gamma         fast          nph #> 1   0     1 0.0001020735 0.0001020735 #> 2   1     0 1.6848546117 1.6848546117 #> 3   0     0 1.0627398613 1.0627398613 #> 4   1     1 0.0033228087 0.0033228087

title: Validation of FastSurvival
text: coxph_fast() returns Pike-Halley Estimator, closed-form approximation Cox partial likelihood maximizer. data moderate hazard ratio two agree closely. report log hazard ratio .
code: fast_hr <- coxph_fast(ovarian$futime, ovarian$fustat, ovarian$rx, control = 1)  ref_cox <- coxph(Surv(futime, fustat) ~ I(rx == 2), data = ovarian)  c(fast = unclass(fast_hr)["coef"], cox = unname(coef(ref_cox))) #> fast.coef       cox  #>  -0.59638  -0.59638

title: Validation of FastSurvival
text: rmst_fast() integrates Kaplan-Meier survival curve horizon. compare two-group RMST difference survRM2::rmst2().
code: library(survRM2)  arm  <- as.integer(ovarian$rx == 2) tau  <- 500  fast_rmst <- rmst_fast(ovarian$futime, ovarian$fustat, ovarian$rx,                        control = 1, tau = tau)  ref_rmst  <- rmst2(time = ovarian$futime, status = ovarian$fustat,                    arm = arm, tau = tau)  c(fast = unclass(fast_rmst)["diff"],   survRM2 = ref_rmst$unadjusted.result[1, 1]) #> fast.diff   survRM2  #>  98.88034  98.88034

title: Validation of FastSurvival
text: milestone_fast() compares Kaplan-Meier survival two groups milestone timepoint. per-group survival probabilities match survfit().
code: tstar <- 500  fast_ms <- milestone_fast(ovarian$futime, ovarian$fustat, ovarian$rx,                           tau = tstar, method = "wald")  fit_g <- survfit(Surv(futime, fustat) ~ rx, data = ovarian) ref_g <- summary(fit_g, times = tstar)  ms_surv <- fast_ms$surv  data.frame(   group    = c("control", "treatment"),   fast     = c(ms_surv["control"], ms_surv["treatment"]),   survival = ref_g$surv,   row.names = NULL ) #>       group      fast  survival #> 1   control 0.5384615 0.5384615 #> 2 treatment 0.6581197 0.6581197

title: Validation of FastSurvival
text: ahsw_fast() computes average hazard survival weight Uno Horiguchi. compare per-group average hazard survAH::ah2(). average hazard ratio cumulative event probability restricted mean survival time, ovarian data, follow-measured days, values order 1e-03 per day groups.
code: library(survAH)  arm <- as.integer(ovarian$rx == 2) tau <- 500  fast_ah <- ahsw_fast(ovarian$futime, ovarian$fustat, ovarian$rx,                      control = 1, tau = tau)  ref_ah  <- ah2(time = ovarian$futime, status = ovarian$fustat,                arm = arm, tau = tau)  data.frame(   quantity = c("AH (control)", "AH (treatment)"),   fast     = unclass(fast_ah)[c("ah.ctrl", "ah.trt")],   survAH   = c(ref_ah$ah["AH (arm0)", "Est."],                ref_ah$ah["AH (arm1)", "Est."]),   row.names = NULL ) #>         quantity        fast      survAH #> 1   AH (control) 0.001235076 0.001235076 #> 2 AH (treatment) 0.000723445 0.000723445

title: Validation of FastSurvival
text: ahr_fast() computes Kalbfleisch-Prentice average hazard ratio two groups restricted interval. reference implementation ahrKM() AHR package, package archived CRAN, check point estimates direct survival-based computation: group’s Kaplan-Meier curve survfit(), integrated form group shares total hazard. point estimates match survival-based reference numerical precision. average hazard ratio additionally cross-checked archived AHR package, reference implementation used Dormuth et al. (2024): two group shares, average hazard ratio, variances, test statistics agree within order 1e-15. external comparison reproducible tools/verify_ahr_fast.R script shipped package sources.
code: tau <- 500  fast_ahr <- ahr_fast(ovarian$futime, ovarian$fustat, ovarian$rx, tau = tau)  g  <- ovarian$rx ev <- c(ovarian$futime[g == 1 & ovarian$fustat == 1],         ovarian$futime[g == 2 & ovarian$fustat == 1]) grid <- sort(unique(c(0, ev[ev <= tau], tau)))  km_on_grid <- function(gi) {   sf <- survfit(Surv(futime, fustat) ~ 1, data = ovarian[g == gi, ])   approxfun(sf$time, sf$surv, method = "constant",             yleft = 1, rule = 2, f = 0)(grid) }  S1  <- km_on_grid(1) S2  <- km_on_grid(2) m   <- length(grid) dS1 <- S1 - c(1, S1[-m]) GL  <- S1[m] * S2[m] ref_theta1 <- -sum(S2 * dS1) / (1 - GL)  data.frame(   quantity  = c("theta (group 1)", "theta (group 2)", "AHR"),   fast      = c(fast_ahr$theta[[1]], fast_ahr$theta[[2]], fast_ahr$ahr),   reference = c(ref_theta1, 1 - ref_theta1, (1 - ref_theta1) / ref_theta1),   row.names = NULL ) #>          quantity      fast reference #> 1 theta (group 1) 0.6965377 0.6965377 #> 2 theta (group 2) 0.3034623 0.3034623 #> 3             AHR 0.4356725 0.4356725

title: Validation of FastSurvival
text: maxcombo_fast() computes max-combo test, extreme set Fleming-Harrington weighted log-rank statistics. nph package provides logrank.maxtest(), whose default weight set FH(0, 0), FH(0, 1), FH(1, 0). request three weights maxcombo_fast() compare component Z-scores. individual Z-scores kept z attribute, signed negative value indicates benefit treatment group package convention. nph orients contrast opposite direction, signs mirrored, compare absolute values. component statistics agree absolute value. final max-combo statistic largest , two-sided test max(abs(Z)) therefore always non-negative. p-values two packages close identical, since use different numerical methods joint distribution (multivariate normal integral , Bonferroni-style combination nph).
code: rho   <- c(0, 0, 1) gamma <- c(0, 1, 0)  fast_mc <- maxcombo_fast(ovarian$futime, ovarian$fustat, ovarian$rx,                          control = 1, side = 2, rho = rho, gamma = gamma)  nph_mc <- nph::logrank.maxtest(ovarian$futime, ovarian$fustat, ovarian$rx)  data.frame(   weight = c("FH(0,0)", "FH(0,1)", "FH(1,0)"),   fast   = abs(attr(fast_mc, "z")),   nph    = abs(nph_mc$tests$z),   row.names = NULL ) #>    weight       fast        nph #> 1 FH(0,0) 1.03089275 1.03089275 #> 2 FH(0,1) 0.01010314 0.01010314 #> 3 FH(1,0) 1.29801950 1.29801950

title: Validation of FastSurvival
text: rmw_fast() computes robust modestly-weighted (rMW) test Magirr Ohrn, maximum standard log-rank statistic single modestly-weighted log-rank statistic. component weighted log-rank test, check two component Z-scores nphRCT, implementation used method’s authors. modestly-weighted component uses survival-threshold parameterization, s_star = 1 recovers standard log-rank test s_star = 0.5 gives modestly-weighted component. max-combo test package orients negative Z toward treatment benefit nphRCT uses opposite sign, compare absolute values. component statistics agree absolute value. null correlation two components, reported corr attribute, matches covariance computed authors’ find_cor routine numerical precision (0.98 ovarian data). combined statistic one-sided p-value follow bivariate normal distribution two components, evaluated mvtnorm::pmvnorm. POPLAR overall-survival data analyzed original article, rmw_fast() reproduces published one-sided p-values 0.0028 log-rank test, 0.0009 modestly-weighted test, 0.0012 rMW test, component correlation 0.97.
code: ov_df <- data.frame(   time  = ovarian$futime,   event = ovarian$fustat,   arm   = factor(ifelse(ovarian$rx == 1, "control", "experimental"),                  levels = c("control", "experimental")) )  fit_rmw <- rmw_fast(ov_df$time, ov_df$event, ov_df$arm,                     control = "control", side = 1, s_star = 0.5)  z_lr_nph <- nphRCT::wlrt(Surv(time, event) ~ arm, data = ov_df,                          method = "mw", s_star = 1)$z z_mw_nph <- nphRCT::wlrt(Surv(time, event) ~ arm, data = ov_df,                          method = "mw", s_star = 0.5)$z  data.frame(   component = c("log-rank (s_star = 1)", "modestly-weighted (s_star = 0.5)"),   fast      = abs(attr(fit_rmw, "z")),   nphRCT    = abs(c(z_lr_nph, z_mw_nph)),   row.names = NULL ) #>                          component      fast    nphRCT #> 1            log-rank (s_star = 1) 1.0308927 1.0308927 #> 2 modestly-weighted (s_star = 0.5) 0.7582546 0.7582546

code: library(FastSurvival) library(survival)  # ---------------------------------------------------------------- # survfit_fast(): Kaplan-Meier at a single time point # ---------------------------------------------------------------- ord <- order(ovarian$futime) t_s <- ovarian$futime[ord] e_s <- ovarian$fustat[ord]  survfit_fast(t_s, e_s, t_eval = 500, conf.type = "log")  # ---------------------------------------------------------------- # survdiff_fast(): log-rank test # ---------------------------------------------------------------- survdiff_fast(ovarian$futime, ovarian$fustat, ovarian$rx,               control = 1, side = 2)  # ---------------------------------------------------------------- # coxph_fast(): hazard ratio via the Pike-Halley Estimator (closed-form) # ---------------------------------------------------------------- coxph_fast(ovarian$futime, ovarian$fustat, ovarian$rx, control = 1)  # ---------------------------------------------------------------- # rmst_fast(): restricted mean survival time (two-group) # ---------------------------------------------------------------- rmst_fast(ovarian$futime, ovarian$fustat, ovarian$rx,           control = 1, tau = 500)  # ---------------------------------------------------------------- # simdata_fast(): clinical trial simulation # ----------------------------------------------------------------  # Two-group trial, simple exponential, no dropout df <- simdata_fast(   nsim     = 1000,   n        = c(100, 100),   a.time   = c(0, 12),   a.rate   = 200 / 12,   e.median = list(18, 24),   seed     = 1 ) head(df)  # Two-group trial, piecewise exponential (delayed treatment effect) df2 <- simdata_fast(   nsim     = 1000,   n        = c(100, 100),   a.time   = c(0, 12),   a.rate   = 200 / 12,   e.hazard = list(c(0.08, 0.08), c(0.08, 0.04)),   e.time   = c(0, 6, Inf),   seed     = 2 )

title: Fast Survival Analysis and Simulation for Clinical Trials
text: typical simulation workflow combines simulation analysis layers. simdata_fast() generates data , analysis_fast() performs looks computes statistics every simulated trial, simsummary_fast() aggregates operating characteristics supplied boundaries: estimation functions can also used directly inside loop. returned object internally numeric vector, stripping class unclass() per-simulation results can combined rbind() ordinary numeric matrix:
code: library(FastSurvival)  # 1. Simulate a two-group trial with a delayed treatment effect df <- simdata_fast(   nsim     = 1000,   n        = c(350, 350),   a.time   = c(0, 12),   a.rate   = 700 / 12,   e.hazard = list(c(0.08, 0.08), c(0.08, 0.04)),   e.time   = c(0, 6, Inf),   d.hazard = list(0.01, 0.01),   seed     = 42 )  # 2. Analyze at two interim looks defined by target event counts res <- analysis_fast(   df, control = 1,   event.looks = c(300, 450),   stat = "logrank", side = 1 )  # 3. Summarize operating characteristics against efficacy boundaries #    (nominal levels from a group-sequential design, e.g. gsDesign) oc <- simsummary_fast(   res,   p.col = "logrank.p",   alpha = c(0.0006, 0.0238) ) oc results <- vector("list", 1000L) for (s in seq_len(1000L)) {   d <- df[df$sim == s, ]   results[[s]] <- coxph_fast(d$tte, d$event, d$group, control = 1) }  hr_mat <- do.call(rbind, lapply(results, unclass)) colMeans(hr_mat)

title: Fast Kalbfleisch-Prentice average hazard ratio for two groups — ahr_fast
code: set.seed(1) n <- 200 time1 <- rexp(n, 0.1) time2 <- rexp(n, 0.18) cens <- rexp(2 * n, 0.05) obs <- pmin(c(time1, time2), cens) status <- as.integer(c(time1, time2) <= cens) group <- rep(c(0, 1), each = n) ahr_fast(obs, status, group, tau = 8) #>  #> Kalbfleisch-Prentice average hazard ratio #> Comparison group '1' vs reference '0' over [0, 8] #>  #>   AHR          : 2.248 #>   95% CI      : 1.699, 2.973 #>   log(AHR)     : 0.81  (se 0.1427) #>   Z (H0: AHR = 1) : 6.317  [theta scale] #>   p-value      : 2.661e-10  [theta scale] #>   (log scale: Z = 5.676, p = 1.379e-08) #>  #> Group shares of total hazard (theta): #>              0        1 #> theta   0.3079   0.6921 #> n     200.0000 200.0000 #>

title: Fast Closed-Form Hazard Ratio Estimation via the Pike-Halley Estimator — coxph_fast
text: Let t_k (k = 1, ..., K) denote distinct observed event times pooled sample. t_k, let n_T_k n_C_k numbers risk treatment control groups just t_k, let O_T_k O_C_k numbers events group, n_k = n_T_k + n_C_k O_k = O_T_k + O_C_k. Define E_T = sum n_T_k O_k / n_k E_C = sum n_C_k O_k / n_k log-rank expected event totals. Pike-Halley Estimator obtained three steps. First, Pike anchor computed theta_0 = (O_T E_C) / (O_C E_T). Second, score U_0, observed information I_0, third-order curvature term J_0 Breslow partial likelihood evaluated eta_0 = log(theta_0): p_k = n_T_k theta_0 / (n_C_k + n_T_k theta_0) U_0 = sum (O_T_k - O_k p_k) I_0 = sum O_k p_k (1 - p_k) J_0 = sum O_k p_k (1 - p_k) (1 - 2 p_k) Third, closed-form Halley correction applied: delta_hat = U_0 / I_0 - J_0 U_0^2 / (2 I_0^3) theta_hat = theta_0 exp(delta_hat) residual error satisfies |theta_hat - theta_Cox| = O_p(n^{-3/2}), three orders magnitude faster O_p(n^{-1/2}) rate Peto Pike, per-call cost approximately thirty times lower iterative Cox solver (Homma, 2025). Wald standard error log scale SE = 1 / sqrt(I_0), I_0 observed information evaluated Pike anchor. quantity used Wald confidence interval reported summary(coxph(...)), based observed information maximum likelihood estimate. Pike anchor lies within O_p(n^{-1/2}) Cox maximum likelihood estimate, difference I_0 information maximum likelihood estimate negligible purpose interval construction. C++ core (pihe_core) accepts pooled sorted data together integer group indicator performs group splitting, -risk counting, per-distinct-event-time accumulation single left--right scan. eliminates rev(cumsum(rev(...))), tapply(), (), diff(), group-split vector copies present pure-R version. returned object class "coxph_fast" named numeric vector length 5. print() method formats result similarly summary(coxph(...)).

code: library(survival)  # Compare coxph_fast with coxph on the ovarian dataset. # coxph() treats rx as numeric with rx=1 as the reference (control), # so set control = 1 for a consistent comparison. fit_fast <- coxph_fast(ovarian$futime, ovarian$fustat, ovarian$rx, control = 1) fit_fast #> Pike-Halley estimator for the hazard ratio (two-group) #>  #> Coefficients: #>          coef exp(coef) se(coef)      z Pr(>|z|) #> group -0.5964    0.5508   0.5868 -1.016     0.31 #>  #> Hazard ratio and 95% Wald confidence interval: #>       exp(coef) exp(-coef) lower .95 upper .95 #> group    0.5508     1.8155    0.1744    1.7399  fit_cox <- summary(coxph(Surv(futime, fustat) ~ rx, data = ovarian)) cat("coxph_fast HR :", fit_fast["exp(coef)"], "\n") #> coxph_fast HR : 0.5508019  cat("coxph      HR :", fit_cox$coefficients[, "exp(coef)"], "\n") #> coxph      HR : 0.5508019   # presorted = TRUE: sort once outside, reuse inside a loop ord <- order(ovarian$futime) coxph_fast(ovarian$futime[ord], ovarian$fustat[ord], ovarian$rx[ord],            control = 1, presorted = TRUE) #> Pike-Halley estimator for the hazard ratio (two-group) #>  #> Coefficients: #>          coef exp(coef) se(coef)      z Pr(>|z|) #> group -0.5964    0.5508   0.5868 -1.016     0.31 #>  #> Hazard ratio and 95% Wald confidence interval: #>       exp(coef) exp(-coef) lower .95 upper .95 #> group    0.5508     1.8155    0.1744    1.7399  # \donttest{ library(microbenchmark) microbenchmark(   coxph_fast = coxph_fast(ovarian$futime, ovarian$fustat, ovarian$rx, 2),   coxph      = coxph(Surv(futime, fustat) ~ rx, data = ovarian),   times = 1000 ) #> Unit: microseconds #>        expr    min      lq      mean  median      uq     max neval cld #>  coxph_fast   25.4   66.10  108.5324   95.95  129.15  1854.1  1000  a  #>       coxph 1070.4 2213.85 2780.0784 2700.15 3046.15 20196.7  1000   b # }

what: Core restricted mean survival time computation (C++ backend) — rmst_core
title: Core restricted mean survival time computation (C++ backend) — rmst_core
text: Internal C++ function computes restricted mean survival time (RMST) Greenwood-type variance single group horizon tau. single left--right scan sorted data accumulates Kaplan-Meier product area survival step function, storing one summary per distinct event time (cumulative area left time Greenwood increment). second short pass saved summaries forms variance sum squared right-side areas weighted Greenwood increments. Accepts event vector numeric (double) vector avoid .integer() copy R. intended called directly users; use rmst_fast() instead.

Returns NA_real_ values (still   class "rmst_fast") n zero single-group mode.

title: Fast Restricted Mean Survival Time (Single Group or Two-Group Comparison) — rmst_fast
text: RMST area Kaplan-Meier curve 0 tau: RMST(tau) = integral [0, tau] S(u) du. variance follows Greenwood-type estimator Var[RMST] = sum_{t_i <= tau} A_i^2 d_i / (n_i (n_i - d_i)), A_i = integral [t_i, tau] S(u) du area right event time t_i. matches restricted mean reported survfit survRM2 package. group supplied, treatment group level group equal control. Writing treatment control RMST r1 r0 variances v1 v0, difference contrast diff = r1 - r0 Var[diff] = v1 + v0, using independence two groups. ratio contrast formed log scale delta method, Var[log(r1 / r0)] = v1 / r1^2 + v0 / r0^2, confidence interval exponentiated back ratio scale. match unadjusted contrasts reported survRM2. presorted = TRUE, input vectors assumed sorted ascending order time; splitting groups preserves ascending order within group, re-sorting performed. presorted = FALSE (default), sorting handled internally. simulation loops data generated sorted order, presorted = TRUE avoids one O(n log n) pass.

title: Fast Restricted Mean Survival Time (Single Group or Two-Group Comparison) — rmst_fast
text: Royston, P., & Parmar, M. K. B. (2013). Restricted mean survival time: alternative hazard ratio design analysis randomized trials time--event outcome. BMC Medical Research Methodology, 13, 152. Uno, H., Claggett, B., Tian, L., et al. (2014). Moving beyond hazard ratio quantifying -group difference survival analysis. Journal Clinical Oncology, 32(22), 2380-2385.

title: Fast Restricted Mean Survival Time (Single Group or Two-Group Comparison) — rmst_fast
code: set.seed(42) t_raw <- rexp(100, rate = 1 / 10) e_raw <- rbinom(100, 1, 0.7)  # Single-group RMST rmst_fast(t_raw, e_raw, tau = 10) #> Restricted mean survival time (single-group) #>  #>   tau = 10 #>  #>            RMST std.err lower 95% upper 95% #> tau = 10 7.0341  0.3452    6.3575    7.7107  # Single-group, pre-sorted (sort once, reuse in a loop) ord <- order(t_raw) rmst_fast(t_raw[ord], e_raw[ord], tau = 10, presorted = TRUE) #> Restricted mean survival time (single-group) #>  #>   tau = 10 #>  #>            RMST std.err lower 95% upper 95% #> tau = 10 7.0341  0.3452    6.3575    7.7107  # Two-group comparison (difference and ratio) set.seed(7) n <- 200 time  <- c(rexp(n, 0.10), rexp(n, 0.07)) event <- rbinom(2 * n, 1, 0.8) group <- rep(0:1, each = n) rmst_fast(time, event, group = group, control = 0, tau = 10) #> Restricted mean survival time (two-group) #>  #>   tau = 10,  control = 0 #>  #>             RMST #> control   6.7572 #> treatment 7.8384 #>  #>                                    Est. lower 95% upper 95%     z Pr(>|z|)    #> difference (treatment - control) 1.0813    0.4094    1.7531 3.154  0.00161 ** #> ratio (treatment / control)      1.1600    1.0572    1.2729 3.133  0.00173 ** #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1  # \donttest{ # Validation against survRM2 if (requireNamespace("survRM2", quietly = TRUE)) {   survRM2::rmst2(time, event, group, tau = 10)$unadjusted.result } #>                           Est. lower .95 upper .95           p #> RMST (arm=1)-(arm=0) 1.0812605 0.4094144 1.7531066 0.001608610 #> RMST (arm=1)/(arm=0) 1.1600165 1.0571618 1.2728783 0.001727894 #> RMTL (arm=1)/(arm=0) 0.6665677 0.5119289 0.8679185 0.002596875 # }

title: Core robust modestly-weighted log-rank computation (C++ backend) — rmw_core
text: standard log-rank numerator U_lr = sum (d1 - e1) variance V_lr = sum var_d, d1 number treatment-group events event time, e1 = d n1 / n expected count, var_d = d n1 n0 (n - d) / (n^2 (n - 1)) hypergeometric variance d1. modestly-weighted numerator U_mw = sum w (d1 - e1) variance V_mw = sum w^2 var_d. standard log-rank weight one, null covariance two numerators reduces C = sum w var_d. modestly-weighted weight capped 1 / s_star, constant, unlike timepoint parameterization used weighted_logrank_core first pass needed quantities accumulate one scan. s_star positive cap treated infinite, giving uncapped 1 / S(t-) weight. Setting s_star = 1 caps weight one, modestly-weighted component equals standard log-rank component two perfectly correlated.

text: time numeric vector follow-times subjects. event integer numeric vector event indicators (1 = event, 0 = censored), aligned time. group vector group labels aligned time. control scalar value indicating level group represents control group. side integer, either 1 2. side = 1, returns standardized log-rank statistic (Z-score), defined (O_1 - E_1) / sqrt(V_1) treatment group, Z-score negative treatment group fewer events expected (protective treatment effect). side = 2, returns chi-square statistic (Z^2). presorted logical value. TRUE, time, event, group (strata supplied) assumed already sorted required order, internal order() call skipped. FALSE (default), sorting handled internally. See Details required order stratified case. strata optional vector stratum labels aligned time. NULL (default), ordinary log-rank test computed behavior identical earlier versions function. supplied, stratified log-rank test computed, matching survdiff strata() term. type supports equality comparison accepted. May combined non-default weight obtain stratified weighted log-rank test. weight character string naming weight scheme. "logrank" (default) ordinary unweighted log-rank test reproduces behavior earlier versions function exactly. "fh" Fleming-Harrington G(rho, gamma) test weight S(t-)^rho (1 - S(t-))^gamma. "mwlrt" modestly-weighted log-rank test Magirr Burman weight 1 / max(S(t-), S(t_star)). "gehan" Gehan-Breslow test weight equal -risk count, "tarone-ware" uses square root -risk count. S(t-) left-continuous pooled Kaplan-Meier estimate just prior event time. rho numeric Fleming-Harrington first parameter, used weight = "fh". Defaults 0. gamma numeric Fleming-Harrington second parameter, used weight = "fh". Defaults 0. pair rho = 0, gamma = 0 reproduces ordinary log-rank test, rho = 0, gamma = 1 Fleming-Harrington G(0, 1) test delayed effects. t_star single non-negative numeric value, timepoint modestly-weighted log-rank test. Required weight = "mwlrt". weight capped 1 / S(t_star), S(t_star) smallest pooled Kaplan-Meier value t_star. value 0 yields ordinary log-rank test.

title: Fast Kaplan-Meier Survival Probability at a Specified Time Point — survfit_fast
text: Kaplan-Meier estimate time t_eval defined product-limit estimator evaluated largest observed event time less equal t_eval. t_eval smaller first observed event time, S(t) = 1 standard error zero. standard error estimated Greenwood's formula: SE[S(t)] = S(t) * sqrt(sum_{t_i <= t, d_i > 0} d_i / (n_i * (n_i - d_i))) d_i number events n_i number risk time t_i. output field std.err follows convention survfit, reports SE[S(t)] / S(t) (.e., standard error log scale) conf.type != "plain", SE[S(t)] conf.type = "plain". function always returns SE[S(t)] (standard error survival scale). S(t_eval) = 0 (subjects experienced event t_eval), standard error zero confidence interval collapses [0, 0], consistent survfit. presorted = TRUE (default), t_sorted e_sorted assumed sorted ascending order time. presorted = FALSE, vectors sorted internally computation. Three confidence interval types supported via conf.type: "plain": Linear interval survival scale,     S(t) +/- z * SE. bounds clipped [0, 1]. "log": Interval log scale (default     survfit),     S(t) * exp(+/- z * SE / S(t)). "log-log": Interval complementary log-log scale,     S(t)^exp(+/- z * SE / (S(t) * log(S(t)))). returned object class "survfit_fast" named numeric vector length 4 evaluation time t_eval, confidence level conf.int, confidence interval type conf.type stored attributes. print() method formats result similarly print(summary(survival::survfit(...))).

code: set.seed(42) t_raw <- rexp(100, rate = 1 / 10) e_raw <- rbinom(100, 1, 0.7)  # presorted = TRUE (default): sort once outside, reuse inside a loop ord <- order(t_raw) t_s <- t_raw[ord] e_s <- e_raw[ord] survfit_fast(t_s, e_s, t_eval = 10, conf.type = "plain") #> Kaplan-Meier survival estimate (single time point) #>  #>        survival std.err lower 95% upper 95% #> t = 10   0.4744  0.0543    0.3679    0.5809 #>  #>  Confidence interval type: plain survfit_fast(t_s, e_s, t_eval = 10, conf.type = "log") #> Kaplan-Meier survival estimate (single time point) #>  #>        survival std.err lower 95% upper 95% #> t = 10   0.4744  0.0543     0.379    0.5938 #>  #>  Confidence interval type: log survfit_fast(t_s, e_s, t_eval = 10, conf.type = "log-log") #> Kaplan-Meier survival estimate (single time point) #>  #>        survival std.err lower 95% upper 95% #> t = 10   0.4744  0.0543    0.3651    0.5759 #>  #>  Confidence interval type: log-log  # presorted = FALSE: sort internally, convenient for one-off calls survfit_fast(t_raw, e_raw, t_eval = 10, presorted = FALSE) #> Kaplan-Meier survival estimate (single time point) #>  #>        survival std.err lower 95% upper 95% #> t = 10   0.4744  0.0543     0.379    0.5938 #>  #>  Confidence interval type: log  # Validation against survival::survfit library(survival) fit <- survfit(Surv(t_raw, e_raw) ~ 1, conf.type = "plain") summary(fit, times = 10) #> Call: survfit(formula = Surv(t_raw, e_raw) ~ 1, conf.type = "plain") #>  #>  time n.risk n.event survival std.err lower 95% CI upper 95% CI #>    10     37      46    0.474  0.0543        0.368        0.581

title: FastSurvival 0.1.0
text: CRAN release: 2026-05-27 Initial release. Core computations implemented C++ via Rcpp use inside large simulation loops. survfit_fast(): single-time-point Kaplan-Meier estimator Greenwood standard error plain / log / log-log confidence intervals. C++ backend locates evaluation cutoff via binary search accumulates Kaplan-Meier product Greenwood variance sum single scan event positions. Returns object class "survfit_fast" print() method. survdiff_fast(): log-rank test returning one-sided Z-score two-sided chi-square statistic. C++ backend uses two-pointer merge scan pooled sorted vectors, eliminating rank construction, tabulate(), reverse cumulative sum operations standard implementation. Returns object class "survdiff_fast" print() method. coxph_fast(): closed-form hazard ratio estimator via Pike-Halley Estimator method Wald confidence interval. C++ backend performs group splitting, -risk counting, per-distinct-event-time accumulation single pass. Returns object class "coxph_fast" print() method. simdata_fast(): clinical trial data simulator supporting one- two-group designs, piecewise uniform accrual, simple piecewise exponential survival dropout times. C++ backends handle piecewise sampling two-group interleaving, random number generation uses dqrng.
